title: National poverty line vs. GDP per capita
subtitle: The horizontal line indicates the [International Poverty Line](#dod:international-poverty-line), set by the World Bank to be representative of national definitions of poverty adopted by low-income countries.
note: This data is expressed in [international-$](#dod:int_dollar_abbreviation) at 2021 prices to account for inflation and differences in living costs between countries.
caption:
National poverty line vs. GDP per capita

The horizontal line indicates the International Poverty Line, set by the World Bank to be representative of national definitions of poverty adopted by low-income countries.

This scatter plot compares harmonized national poverty lines against GDP per capita for various countries, with both axes plotted on a logarithmic scale. The horizontal axis represents GDP per capita in international dollars at 2021 prices, ranging from $2,000 to over $100,000, while the vertical axis shows the national poverty line in international dollars, spanning from $2 to above $20. A horizontal dashed line marks the International Poverty Line at $3. Countries are color-coded by income group: red for low income, green for lower-middle income, purple for upper-middle income, and blue for high income. Circle sizes correspond to population, with a reference scale showing circles for 1.4 billion and 600 million people. Low-income countries, such as DR Congo, Uganda, Mozambique, and Liberia, cluster in the bottom-left corner with GDP per capita below $2,000 and poverty lines generally between $2 and $5. Lower-middle income nations like India, Nigeria, Kenya, and Indonesia occupy the lower-middle section, with GDP per capita roughly between $5,000 and $10,000 and poverty lines mostly between $2 and $5, though some like Honduras and Sudan sit higher. Upper-middle income countries, including China, Brazil, Mexico, and Turkey, show a wider spread in GDP per capita from roughly $10,000 to $30,000, with poverty lines ranging from approximately $5 to $15. High-income countries, such as the United States, Ireland, Japan, and Poland, are positioned in the top-right, featuring GDP per capita above $30,000 and national poverty lines generally exceeding $10, with the United States and Ireland reaching above $20. The data reveals a positive correlation where higher GDP per capita tends to align with higher national poverty lines.
